header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: Midpoint Review -- Consolidating HR Leadership Language for Canada
lesson.menu_desc: Participle clauses e a linguagem de quem APRESENTA um balanço: takeaway, caveat, trade-off, quick win, track record -- e as 7 estruturas das aulas 1-7 numa apresentação só -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 8 -- Midpoint Review
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 8 -- Midpoint Review

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire. I have your draft deck for the midpoint review in front of me, and I want to change three things before Warren sees it. The first one is slide one. You open with the methodology and the four phases of the assessment, and Warren will stop reading before he reaches anything you found. Put the takeaway on slide one instead. One sentence, before any detail, before any number. The culture here is not broken, it is unheard. That sentence. The second thing is the thirty-eight percent. It is on slide fourteen, and I understand why you put it there, but you have to say it yourself, early, and you have to call it a caveat. A number you announce is a finding. The same number, discovered by somebody else halfway through your deck, is a cover-up, and it will cost you every other number in the review. And the third thing, Danielle. Do not promise him that the new onboarding is working. It might be. The first cohort has not finished, and you do not know yet. Say that the jury is still out, give him a date, and tell him you will have a tangible number in March. He will respect that far more than he would respect a yes that you cannot defend in the spring.

Danielle, Warren here. Claire tells me you are presenting the midpoint tomorrow, so let me save you some time and tell you how I listen. With most reviews, I stop somewhere around slide three. Not because I am rude, but because by slide three I still do not know what the person found. I only know how they went about finding it, and that is their work, not my decision. So here is what I actually want from your eleven minutes. What you found, in one sentence. What it is going to cost me. And what you want me to decide, today, in that room. Anything else is you thinking out loud, and you can do that with Claire. One more thing, and I mean this. If you bring me only good news, I will not believe you. In twenty-two years I have never seen a project at the halfway mark where everything worked, so a leader who tells me that has either not measured it, or has measured it and is not telling me. Bring me the number you fell short of. I would very much rather hear the caveat from you tomorrow than find it for myself in March.

reading.rtitle: Field note: the eleven minutes that decide the other six months
paras: Every People and Culture leader arriving in a new country knows how to do the work. Far fewer know how to present it. The midpoint review is where those two skills stop being the same thing, and where a great deal of excellent work quietly dies. The room is not evaluating your assessment. It is evaluating whether it can spend money on the basis of what you are saying, and it has eleven minutes in which to decide.

This is why the sequence matters more than the content. A senior room does not want the journey. It wants the takeaway, and it wants it in the first sentence, before a single slide. Having spent six months on careful work, the instinct is to show the six months -- the methodology, the phases, the sample. That instinct is fatal. The Chief Operating Officer stops listening at slide three, and he stops listening not because he is rude but because by slide three he still does not know what you found. He only knows how you went about finding it, and that is your work, not his decision.

The second rule is harder, and it is the one that separates a leader from a candidate. Announce your own bad number. If you fell short of a target you promised in June, that sentence belongs in your mouth, early, framed as a caveat -- not on slide fourteen, waiting to be discovered. The arithmetic here is brutal and it is not about honesty for its own sake: a number you volunteer is a finding, and it makes every other number in the deck more believable. The same number, found by somebody else, is a cover-up, and it retroactively contaminates the ones they already accepted. This is also why a leader who brings only good news is never believed. Nobody has ever reached the halfway mark of anything with everything working.

And the last rule: say what you do not know. On the things that have not finished, the jury is still out, and the executive move is to name a date rather than manufacture a conclusion. None of this is modesty. A leader who names the limit of her own data is a leader whose data can be trusted the next time she brings some -- and, in a country where nobody has seen you work before, that is not a small thing. It is the entire beginning of a track record.

gist.prompt: Read once, for the main idea. Which sentence says what this text is actually arguing?
choices: Leaders whose English is not yet precise enough will lose a senior room no matter how good the underlying work is.
A midpoint review is judged on sequence and candor, not on the quality of the work: the takeaway goes first, and the bad number is volunteered rather than discovered.
The bad numbers in a review should be placed late in the deck, so that the room has already accepted the good ones.
The main problem with most midpoint reviews is that they contain too many slides for the time available.

items: According to the text, the room at a midpoint review is mainly evaluating the quality of the assessment itself.
It is evaluating whether it can spend money on the basis of what you are saying -- and it has eleven minutes to decide.
The writer says the COO stops listening at slide three because he is impatient and rude.
He stops because by slide three he still does not know what you FOUND -- only how you went about finding it, which is your work and not his decision.
The text argues that you should announce your own bad number early, in your own words.
A number you volunteer is a finding; the same number, discovered by someone else, is a cover-up that contaminates the numbers they had already accepted.
According to the writer, a leader who brings only good news is seen as strong and competent.
That leader is never believed: nobody has ever reached the halfway mark of anything with everything working.
The writer treats saying 'the jury is still out' as a form of modesty or weakness.
It is the executive move: name a date instead of manufacturing a conclusion. Naming the limit of your own data is how a track record begins.

tips: The takeaway first: the culture here is not broken, it is unheard, and the response rate is the evidence. Everything else I say defends that sentence.
never open with the methodology -- he stops listening at slide three

items.situation: "Give me the number you would rather I did not see."
tips: Thirty-eight percent, against the seventy I promised you in June. We fell short of my own target, and that is my caveat: the sample is smaller than I wanted.
volunteer it, do not defend it

items.situation: "So the survey failed and you want me to fund more of it?"
tips: Given what the focus groups told us, I would argue the thirty-eight is itself the finding. A faculty that was surveyed once and never told the outcome does not answer twice.
turn the attack into the evidence -- and hedge the claim, do not shout it
On that one, the jury is still out. The first cohort finishes in February, so I will have a tangible number for you in March, and not before.
name a date; never manufacture a conclusion

items.situation: "Two quick wins is not a strategy. I want the whole framework by Q2."
tips: We can do that, and here is the trade-off: the baseline gets taken after the change rather than before, which costs us the ability to prove that any of it worked.
do not refuse and do not simply agree -- name the cost and leave the decision with him

items.situation: "Why should I believe any of this? Nobody here has seen you work before."
tips: You should not, yet. Having run four focus groups and one survey, what I have is preliminary, and I have told you exactly where it is thin. Judge me in March against the number I just gave you.
no track record yet -- so the caveat IS the credibility
